wrapUp: The 2026 World Cup is under way after Mexico beat South Africa in a fiery opening night at the Azteca, but the treatment room still dominates the narrative. Brazil and Morocco meet on 13 June with both sides depleted: Neymar is set to miss the Seleção's curtain-raiser, while the Atlas Lions have lost Ezzalzouli and Aguerd. Co-host Canada open without captain Alphonso Davies, though Moïse Bombito is fit, and the United States report all 26 players available for their Paraguay opener. Argentina have confirmed Marcos Senesi as Leonardo Balerdi's replacement. Each story below links to the specific report it is drawn from; squad and fitness details reflect the latest available reporting as of 12 June 2026.

sections.heading: Injuries and fitness doubts
sections.description: Players ruled out, late replacements and day-to-day concerns that could change starting line-ups and squad depth.

stories.headline: Neymar set to miss Brazil opener as Rodrygo, Estêvão and Wesley are ruled out
stories.summary: Carlo Ancelotti's final 26 includes Neymar despite a grade-two calf injury, but the forward has not returned to training and is certain to miss the Morocco curtain-raiser on 13 June. Brazil have already lost Rodrygo (ACL), Estêvão (hamstring) and Éder Militão before the tournament, while right-back Wesley was ruled out with a thigh injury after the Egypt friendly, with Atalanta midfielder Éderson called up as his replacement. Vinícius Júnior and Raphinha remain, yet the Seleção look thinner in attack and defence than their pedigree suggests.

stories.headline: Davies confirmed out as Canada host Bosnia without their captain
stories.summary: Alphonso Davies will miss co-hosts Canada's World Cup opener against Bosnia-Herzegovina in Toronto on 12 June as he continues to recover from a hamstring injury sustained playing for Bayern Munich in May. Jesse Marsch confirmed the captain's absence on the eve of kick-off, though an MRI showed encouraging progress and Marsch believes Davies can feature later in the group stage, possibly from the third match against Switzerland. Centre-back Moïse Bombito, returning from a broken leg, is available after winning his fitness race.

stories.headline: Mexico win opener but lose Montes to red card after fitness gamble
stories.summary: Javier Aguirre's side beat South Africa 2-0 in the tournament's opening match at the Azteca, yet the night was marred by three red cards, the most in a World Cup opener. César Montes, one of four players Aguirre had been managing through muscle complaints, was sent off in stoppage time and will miss the South Korea fixture on 18 June. Edson Álvarez, Luis Chávez and Alexis Vega all featured, suggesting Aguirre's pre-tournament fitness gambles largely paid off for the first game.

stories.headline: Morocco lose Ezzalzouli and Aguerd on eve of Brazil opener
stories.summary: FIFA confirmed late squad changes after forward Abde Ezzalzouli suffered a knee ligament injury colliding with team-mate Chadi Riad in the Norway friendly, and centre-back Nayef Aguerd failed to recover from a long-standing groin problem. Amine Sbai and Marwane Saadane were called up as replacements. Morocco had hoped both could be nursed through the group stage but replaced them before the 24-hour registration deadline ahead of their Group C opener against Brazil on 13 June.

stories.headline: Netherlands lose Timber and Simons as Verbruggen faces race for Japan opener
stories.summary: Jurrien Timber has been ruled out with a groin strain despite playing in Arsenal's Champions League final, joining ACL casualties Xavi Simons and Jerdy Schouten plus Matthijs de Ligt, who failed to recover from a back problem. Ronald Koeman must also monitor goalkeeper Bart Verbruggen, who missed training with a hip issue after a collision against Uzbekistan. The Dutch open Group F against Japan in Dallas on 14 June without several planned starters.

stories.headline: South Korea fear Kim Tae-hyun could miss entire group stage
stories.summary: Starting centre-back Kim Tae-hyun injured his ankle in training and may sit out all three Group A fixtures against Czechia, Mexico and South Africa. Hong Myung-bo's side had already lost Jo Yu-min during the warm-up window. With Kim Min-jae the only established aerial presence left, South Korea's defensive plan against Mexico's direct play is under strain before a ball is kicked.

stories.headline: United States report full squad fit for Paraguay opener
stories.summary: Johnny Cardoso was ruled out after an ankle sprain requiring surgery, but Mauricio Pochettino confirmed on 11 June that all 26 remaining players are available for selection against Paraguay. Chris Richards, who missed warm-up matches with torn ankle ligaments, returned to full training and said he felt ready for the opener. Pochettino must still decide whether to start a defender who has lacked competitive minutes, with Group D also featuring Australia and Türkiye.

stories.headline: Tuchel admits Saka is unlikely to finish every England match
stories.summary: Bukayo Saka arrives at the World Cup managing an Achilles issue he played through at the end of Arsenal's season. Thomas Tuchel said the winger is available but not at 100% and may need rotation across the group stage, raising questions over England's right flank in Group L against Croatia, Ghana and Panama. Noni Madueke is the natural deputy, though Tuchel has other tactical options if Saka's minutes must be capped.

stories.headline: Spain manage Rodri scare and Yamal race as Fermín López misses out
stories.summary: Euro 2024 winners head into Group H without Barcelona midfielder Fermín López, who fractured a metatarsal in May. Luis de la Fuente also faces a delicate balancing act around Rodri, who survived a training-ground tackle from Gavi days before the opener but carries a recent ACL history, and Lamine Yamal, who was kept from heavy contact drills while completing his return from injury. Spain's control-based system depends on that spine staying intact across a demanding 48-team schedule.

stories.headline: Ghana's World Cup balance fractured by Kudus absence
stories.summary: Tottenham playmaker Mohammed Kudus was ruled out after a quad injury and rehabilitation setback, removing Ghana's main ball-carrier and press-resistant creator. Carlos Queiroz also lost defensive options to injury and handed a late call-up to Derrick Luckassen. Without Kudus driving transitions, Ghana may rely more heavily on Thomas Partey and deep defensive blocks in Group L, where they face England and Croatia.

sections.heading: Squad selection and tactical reshuffles
sections.description: Omissions, late call-ups and manager decisions that reveal how teams plan to navigate the expanded format.

stories.headline: Argentina lose Balerdi as Senesi confirmed as replacement
stories.summary: Leonardo Balerdi suffered a soleus muscle tear in training and the AFA confirmed he would miss the World Cup. On 12 June, the federation announced that Marcos Senesi would take his place, ending speculation that Lionel Scaloni might recall a midfielder instead. Senesi, who has joined Tottenham on a free transfer, adds cover alongside Cristian Romero, Lisandro Martínez and Nicolás Otamendi. Argentina open Group J against Algeria on 16 June.

sections.heading: Disruption and preparation issues
sections.description: Travel, visa and logistical problems that could affect match readiness rather than just headlines.

stories.headline: Iraq squad members face lengthy US border checks
stories.summary: Striker Aymen Hussein was detained for nearly seven hours at Chicago O'Hare before being admitted, while team photographer Talal Salah was held for more than ten hours and ultimately denied entry. Iraq open Group I against France on 16 June. Extended border processing for staff and players adds uncertainty to a squad already tasked with navigating a group containing France, Senegal and Norway.

stories.headline: Haiti receive late visa for their only domestically based player
stories.summary: Woodensky Pierre, the sole member of Haiti's squad living in Haiti, was granted a US visa at the eleventh hour after earlier uncertainty. Haiti face Brazil in Group C on 13 June. Any delay for a player without the same club infrastructure as team-mates based in Europe would have further weakened an already heavy underdog.

stories.headline: Iran base camp in Mexico and shuttle flights for US fixtures
stories.summary: Iran relocated their training base from Arizona to Tijuana and plan to fly into the United States only for matches, after several delegation members were initially denied visas. Players received US visas roughly ten days before their Group G opener against Belgium, but the disrupted pre-tournament routine is far from ideal. FIFA said it worked behind the scenes to ensure Iran could compete, yet the arrangement adds travel fatigue to an already demanding group.

sections.heading: Analysis and tournament outlook
sections.description: Deeper previews on favourites, group dynamics and how the 48-team format changes the path to the final.

stories.headline: Group C opener pits depleted Brazil against reshaped Morocco
stories.summary: Pre-tournament analysis had flagged Group C as one of the toughest pools, and the opening fixture has been further reshaped by injuries on both sides. Brazil arrive without Neymar for the curtain-raiser and minus Rodrygo, Estêvão and Wesley, while Morocco have replaced Ezzalzouli and Aguerd with less familiar names. Morocco's semi-final pedigree from Qatar 2022 still makes them a credible test for Ancelotti's rebuilt attack, but both managers may need to lean on squad depth earlier than planned.

stories.headline: Opta supercomputer makes Spain favourites in 25,000 simulations
stories.summary: Opta Analyst ran 25,000 pre-tournament simulations and found Spain winning 16.1% of them, ahead of France (13%), England (11.2%) and Argentina (10.4%). The model also projects every group, highlighting favourable draws for Argentina in Group J and a competitive Group D where the United States are narrow favourites at 32.8%. With 32 of 48 teams reaching the knockouts, the analysis stresses that squad depth and rotation capacity matter more than in previous editions.

stories.headline: BBC analysis points to Norway and Morocco as sides to watch
stories.summary: BBC Sport's pre-tournament guide identifies Spain as favourites after a near-flawless qualifying campaign, with France, Argentina and Brazil in the chasing pack. Norway are highlighted as a surprise package built around Erling Haaland's 16 qualifying goals, while Morocco are noted for an unbeaten qualifying record and strong AFCON form. Japan's defensive record in Asian qualifying (three goals conceded in 16 matches) and Colombia's wins over Brazil and Argentina are also flagged as reasons the expanded field may produce upsets.
